item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for the picture getting angry, pointing, and crying. Old SCP but still adding my two cents.

Along those lines I had to comment that no one's mentioning the possible exposure of SCP-XXX. Was it implied that it could be SCP-096 due to the "likeness" line? SCP-096 isn't listed as being at any Site, therefor it could certainly be at Site-15! The thought of an addicting meme showing SCP-096 to the world sure seems like a long and terrifying End-of-the-World Scenario.

To agree with others, the weeb in me still wishes it was an anomalous anime girl loose in the internet causing trouble. But an organization backing her isn't too bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rest of the article were interesting, but I'm really not a fan of the email. Agree with the previous post that it would've been better if it was "more butthurt and less moralizing." The description of the Ackermann Protocol wasn't thrilling, either, although I don't see anything wrong with keeping it.

No vote.
I didn't understand a lick of the technobabble section, but it was still entertaining. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a hardcore math nerd, not to mention anime nerd who finds this to be an amazing critique of how stupid many anime fans can be, I am pretty much obligated to upvote this!

I've really gotta learn more Graph Theory!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been on Danbooru too many times now that the first thing I notice is how badly drawn the girl is. However, it's hard to find well drawn original CC-compliant animesque images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks everyone!

Thanks Wuz for the detailed analysis, I'll admit I don't know japanese although I tried. I'll definitely think about making the article more accurate.

Also to everyone regarding the Ackermann Protocol: I had actually named it after the Ackermann function (the first known total computable function that's not simply recursive). I didn't even realize the Attack on Titan connection, but that's bonus points I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Index reference…I FOUND ONE!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… Maybe the name on the page should have the untranslated title or something? Because I was like, "I think it's a Raildex ref… But is it?", but with those changes, or something close to them, the connection is a lot clearer.

Still wondering what Celeramis is…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel guilty for awarding a +1, solely because it explains why I get irrelevant weeaboo shit when I search up something.

I'm not a math wiz, so I can't really judge the Ackerman Protocol - I found it interesting, personally. As with Gaffney, I'm not a fan of the new GoI that's hostile to the SCP Foundation due to its dickery, but at the same time, I'd like to see more of whatever this GoI's gimmick is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "Ackerman Protocol" was a horrible and boring way to start out a document, the anomaly itself was pretty meh, and stapling on a note from Your Friendly Neighborhood GoI That Just Happens To Know How To Easily Contact The Foundation made the idea somehow less compelling. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the fact that the protocol is called Ackermann. I'm willing to bet that's a reference to a certain giant slaying female badass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I was on SCP-JP with this page (http://ja.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1362327/scp-2223), and I noted that the users there apparently noted a problem with the way the author romanized the Japanese name of A Certain Mathematical Conjecture, and several more regarding word stylistic choices. With my basic skills in Japanese, some Google translation and some research, I had managed to get a grasp of what is going on:

The original names of the A Certain Magical series used some Alternate Character Readings for the final words in the titles (Index, Railgun). Basically, they are represented as Kanji (禁書目録, 超電磁砲), but had katakana English readings over them (インデックス Indekkusu, レールガン Reirugan), and they're supposed to pronounced that way. (Yes, in English, the exact same words as they are in English)

So to fit the style that they use, the way the romanized English title should have been written is To Aru Suugakuteki no Conjecture (or To Aru Suugakuteki no Konjekuchaa (コンジェクチャー) if you're feeling like using a more direct and academic style of romanization, but probably won't fit with the loose tone of the email).

That is one part of it.

Suugakuteki involves three kanji (数学的), while the original light novel titles had two kanji characterss after "To Aru" and before "no" (魔術 and 科学 respectively). In addition, the 的 character has some grammatical problems, which I am very sure exists but I cannot describe since my skills with Japanese are not that good yet.

SCP-JP changed Suugakuteki into Suuri (数理), which had the same meaning of mathematics but had only two kanji characters.

And I'm not done yet.

Okusetsu is only two Kanji characters long (臆説), which doesn't fit the style of four-Kanji-characters-with-English-pronounciation-over-them that the light novels used.

On SCP-JP, they used 当て推量, (Toutesuiryou) which also meant conjecture and sort of fitted the four-characters style.

So after Translation Correction　by SCP-JP, the fictional light novel's title is とある数理の当て推量（コンジェクチャー）, where the things in the brackets go over 当て推量 and is supposed to be pronounced as "Conjecture".

Romanize it, and you get To Aru Suuri no Conjecture, which is stylistically more consistent with the other A Certain Magical light novels.

The English name stays the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "moe"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it wasn't actually technobabble in the slightest. That was all completely legitimate linear algebra and good ol' commonplace mathematics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like the idea of a GoI that's just some anime fans having a pout because the Foundation banned them for image-spam. I feel like it would be better if the email had a little more butthurt and less moralizing.

Upvoted, in any case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I respect the heavy use of math, even if I don't understand it.
Also this is a good way to have an anime-flavored SCP. Upvote'd.
A little iffy on the new GOI, but I'd like to see if it evolves any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is…is it bad I started telling people about this already…have I been affected!
No but seriously, I enjoyed this greatly. Even checked google a few times for fun.
I too love the reference to the To aru series. I almost searched for it until I saw the footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely an interesting scip. Also, is Dr. Ackerman a Shingeki no Kyojin reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. Curious, was the Raildex reference picked because one of the leads had technopathic abilities or was that just incidental?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As mentioned in Addendum 2, the Foundation has embedded agents in these major companies. They probably make sure that the code isn't taken out.

If you're referring to the algorithm itself - it is based on some research papers I've read, although obviously if you stare hard enough at it with enough background you'll find a rather underwhelming surprise, since SCP-2223 doesn't exist in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As you presented it, the modification to the search algorithms of major search engines isn't going to work. Other coders are going to see the changes to the algorithm, analyze it, see that (from their point of view) it doesn't accomplish anything useful, and then get rid of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mathematics brought me in, but the email pushed me back out. Mysterious groups vaguely opposing the Foundation are boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your mathematics caused physical injury to my eyes. It is for this reason that I must upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guess I just missed that. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a mention: "No relevant online matches were found for either the English or Japanese titles of the anime referenced."

Not asking you to change your vote, just pointing out a factual thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This ended up going in the most boring possible direction for me. Not feeling this, sorry.

Also, why is there not even so much as a mention of the foundation investigating the anime series mentioned in the email? Seems like they'd get right on something like that as soon as they would hear about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good pop-culture skip, from where I sit. I was a little iffy about the anime image (it seems people are always trying to find reasons to use drawn art as article images, but this is when it works), but what hits me about this is that containment is compromised by Google's continuous "improvements".

That said, I'm of two minds about the last addendum. On the one hand, making another GoI. (And they're basically anime AWCY.) On the other, I'd rather a GoI have knowledge of the Foundation — they're on the same "level", essentially, in the anomalous world — than a skip slowly figure out what's keeping it from doing its thing as it gets madder and madder. I mean, that's been done.

So I guess that means I don't care about the addendum and will upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The techobabble kinda bored me and the "hook" didn't do much for me, but I can't bring myself to downvote an SCP that feels more like a professionally written document than any other SCP I've ever read, ever.

Neutral voted based on technical merit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the reference to To Aru Majutsu and fourthing the sentiments above. A sentient image of an anime girl (that may not be existent) who craves for attention does feel more interesting than tying it in to an identified group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again, I don't see the point of the addendum. Seconding Scantron in that there is nothing wrong with posting an interesting anomaly with no attached special groups of interest or (not that you included it) internal Foundation soapboxing. That's not a hook. I would go out on a limb and say it makes the writing itself seem insecure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This sent me down a rabbit hole of powerpoints and papers on adjacency matrices and spectral graph theory, which was really fun. I love when I can learn from an SCP.

+1 for going above and beyond with the detail- that really sealed it for me. I'm with Scantron on the Celeramis thing, though. If this is due to a group with knowledge of the Foundation, it doesn't really feel anomalous so much as the work of a clever hacker who likes to mess around with the Foundation. This would be a lot creepier if it acted on its own volition. Still, really cool concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What does "irreducible closed subset" mean, in human-readable terms?

A set is irreducible if you can't define it as the union of other sets and it's closed if it contains its limits (in other words if the numbers at the beginning and end of the set are part of it instead of the set just getting infinitesimally close to them).

Does it have a meaning outside of the realm of math?

Not really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things:

  1. What does "irreducible closed subset" mean, in human-readable terms? Does it have a meaning outside of the realm of math?
  2. I don't like where you went with this, making it the result of the endeavors of some group with Foundation knowledge. I liked this more when it was just an anime girl that was mysteriously showing up high in the search results.

No vote, because I love the thoroughness and creativity of this, but I can't stand by it with those features intact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never really been one for intensive math-based SCPs. I get the feeling that there is something clever going on here with the containment procedures and the part at the end but it's over my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I would like it a lot more if the picture itself was an anomaly and was reposting itself (which is where I thought it was going) but the culprit taking the time to tell the Foundation they need to watch his or her favorite anime made me smile. Captures anime fans pretty well.

Also nice reference to the A Certain Magical series.

I'll upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(for the record)

Removed mention of "waifu". Although I had people review the last addendum, it was looked at fewer times than the rest of the article and the issue was never brought up. I agree with you on that matter now that you bring it up, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for two reasons, and this is because I like what you did with everything else about this;

  1. The image. I can't take this seriously because of the image.1
  2. Using "waifu" destroys immersion completely for me as well. This is mainly what I'm downvoting for.

Otherwise, I like this a ton, and I'm glad you figured out what to do with this.

Footnotes
1. OK, admittedly, the image doesn't bother me as much. But it's pretty fucking silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image of the anime girl was taken from "Elliot Benjamin" under a Creative Commons license (Link: https://flic.kr/p/r71oWw). Changes were made in that the image was edited into a screenshot of Google Image Search results. The Google Image Search screenshotted was filtered to "Labeled for noncommercial use with modification".

Special thanks to Riemann, Djoric, TobiasTheTapir, A Random Day, and Decibelle (as well as others in the chat) for detailed and helpful critiques.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see you figured out your image dilemma.
A very cool thing, +1
